title: Terminal Mental Models, Help, and Evidence · Transfer Runbook
summary: Investigate an unfamiliar Linux workstation without guessing, changing host state, or treating copied commands as explanations. The scenario, data, constraints, and failure modes differ from the workshop. Planned artifact: Produce an independent changed-context version of a terminal orientation and command-evidence notebook with automated and judgment evidence. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Distinguish terminal, shell, command, process, kernel, and operating-system responsibilities in an observed interaction.
Parse command names, options, operands, quoting, and exit status before predicting behavior.
Use man pages, help, type, command -V, info, and project documentation to establish command authority and provenance.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

starterFiles.shell:
# linux-basics cumulative operations evidence notebook
# Commands run only in the deterministic stateful terminal simulator.
# Record hypothesis, command, expected signal, observation, conclusion, and recovery.

steps.title: Commit to the mission model
steps.instruction:
lab case — On a lab host, learner starts in /home/learner/lab with Bash 5.3, Coreutils 9.11, and exit status 0. Resolve ./data/../scripts, identify command authority, and separate terminal, shell, process, and kernel evidence.

What must be established before implementation?
steps.why: The decision supplies observable evidence for distinguish terminal, shell, command, process, kernel, and operating-system responsibilities in an observed interaction.

content.value: GNU Coreutils Manual — Controls file, text, identity, process, filesystem, and common utility behavior.

Use the documented misconception as a counterexample target: The terminal window is the Linux shell and executes commands itself.

options.text: Apply the visible technique immediately, omit assumptions, and treat one successful output as complete proof.
